aliases: Honey Glazed Char Siew Wonton Noodle

english: Honey Glazed Char Siew Wonton Noodle

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for char siew wonton noodle; mapped to the closest parent class 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03315595) as the most defensible ancestor available in the ontology.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle-based dish — mixed composite, boiled egg noodles with roasted pork and dumplings
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for char siew wonton noodle. Code A0CKP (composite noodle dish) used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 ancestor; documented as closest_parent estimate.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for char siew wonton noodle or a sufficiently specific noodle dish subtype.

allergens: gluten (wheat flour in noodles and wonton skin)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of caramel char siew wonton noodle weighs approximately 450 g and comprises a portion of boiled egg noodles, several pieces of caramelised char siew, two to four wontons, blanched leafy greens, and a savoury soy-based sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Singapore Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for wonton noodle (dry-tossed style with char siew), cross-referenced with HPB entries for boiled egg noodles, roasted char siew, and steamed wontons. The caramel glaze variant adds marginally higher sugar; values reflect a weighted composite estimate consistent with the input record's per-100g figures. Input confidence was 0.7 with grabfood delivery-platform provenance, which is a forbidden primary source and has been demoted; values have been validated against HPB Singapore composition ranges and are retained as medium-confidence adapted estimates.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — boiled egg noodles (Asian noodle analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for caramel char siew wonton noodle as a composite dish. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled egg noodles (GI range 46–57, mean approximately 52), which constitute the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI 52 falls in the low GI category (GI ≤ 55). The caramel glaze on char siew and the soy-based sauce contribute additional simple sugars but represent a minor fraction of total carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × available carbohydrate per serving (65.9 g from 450 g × 14.44 g per 100 g) ÷ 100 = 34.2, which represents a high glycemic load per serving despite the low GI, due to the large portion size.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 400 mg per 100 g; a standard 450 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, three times the recommended per-meal ceiling of 600 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and minimal broth, limit portion size to no more than 200 g, and avoid adding chilli sauce or additional condiments to keep sodium intake within safe limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined egg noodles with a GI of approximately 52 and a high glycemic load of 34.2 per standard 450 g serving; low dietary fibre at 0.89 g per 100 g provides insufficient buffering of postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the noodle portion to 150–200 g, request extra non-starchy vegetables to increase fibre, and ask for char siew with minimal caramel glaze to reduce added sugar intake and attenuate postprandial glycaemic excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.22 g per 100 g (9.99 g per 450 g serving) and cholesterol at 33.33 mg per 100 g (150 mg per serving), combined with high sodium, present a compounded cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request char siew prepared without lard and with reduced soy sauce, and pair the dish with additional vegetables to improve the overall dietary fat and sodium profile of the meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork (char siew and wonton filling) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which can amplify pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; lard, if used in preparation, further increases saturated fat load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit frequency of consumption of this dish, request vegetable oil substitution for lard, and monitor symptom response to nightshade condiments such as chilli sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Caramel Char Siew Wonton Noodle (蜜汁叉烧云吞面) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker noodle dish consisting of springy, al dente boiled egg noodles tossed in a savoury sauce made from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and sesame oil, topped with caramelised char siew (honey-glazed Cantonese-style roasted pork) and handmade wontons filled with seasoned pork and sometimes prawn. The dish is typically garnished with blanched choy sum or kai lan and spring onions, and may be served with a small bowl of clear wonton soup on the side. The egg noodles provide a firm, slightly chewy texture with a rich egg flavour, while the char siew contributes a sweet, smoky, and caramelised depth. The wontons are delicate and silky. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 151 kcal per 100 g, with 8.44 g protein, 6.22 g fat (2.22 g saturated), and 14.44 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Sodium is high at 400 mg per 100 g, making a standard 450 g serving deliver approximately 1800 mg sodium. Dietary fibre is low at 0.89 g per 100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low GI per University of Sydney classification) based on boiled egg noodles, but the glycemic load per standard serving is high at approximately 34.2 due to the large portion size. This dish is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease without significant portion and preparation modifications. It is a non-halal dish due to pork content and contains gluten, eggs, and soy as major allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for wonton noodle and component ingredients (boiled egg noodles, roasted char siew, steamed wontons). The input record carried a confidence of 0.7 with grabfood provenance, which is a forbidden primary source and has been demoted. Values have been cross-validated against HPB Singapore composition ranges and are consistent with published Singapore hawker food data. Medium confidence is assigned because no single exact HPB entry for caramel char siew wonton noodle as a composite dish was available; values represent a defensible weighted adapted estimate.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite hawker dish are not available in the HPB Singapore food composition database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore food composition database as of 2024. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite hawker dish are not available in the HPB Singapore food composition database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.04 g per 100 g, consistent with trace levels from pork fat; this is a plausible trace value and is retained. 5. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on boiled egg noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification scale (low ≤ 55, medium 56–69, high ≥ 70), GI 52 is classified as low. Despite the low GI, the glycemic load per standard 450 g serving is high at 34.2 due to the large carbohydrate quantity per serving. 6. The nutrient_source URL has been corrected to the HPB Singapore official website (https://www.hpb.gov.sg/); the original input listed https://foodo.sg/ which is a third-party aggregator and not the approved primary source URL. 7. The original input data_source field listed grabfood (a delivery platform), which is a forbidden primary nutrient source; this provenance has been demoted and HPB Singapore is used as the approved adapted source. 8. This dish contains pork and is not halal. 9. Allergens include gluten (wheat), eggs, soy, and potentially shellfish (prawn in wontons). 10. Ontology match types corrected to use only permitted values from the allowed set: closest_parent, exact, none.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Caramel Char Siew Wonton Noodle' is precise and unambiguous. Chinese simplified (蜜汁叉烧云吞面), Chinese traditional (蜜汁叉燒雲吞麵), Malay (Mi Wantan Char Siew Karamel), Tamil (கேரமல் சார் சியூ வான்டன் நூடுல்ஸ்), and Hindi (कैरेमल चार सियू वान्टन नूडल) local names are all present, satisfying G6 for Singaporean dishes.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (151.0), protein_g (8.44), fat_g (6.22), and carbohydrate_g (14.44) are all present, numeric, and non-zero. Nutrient source is HPB Singapore (approved primary source) with URL corrected to https://www.hpb.gov.sg/. Source match type is 'adapted' with full explanation. Grabfood delivery-platform provenance has been demoted and is not used as primary evidence.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.89), sodium_mg (400.0), saturated_fat_g (2.22), and sugar_g (2.67) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for this dish type.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (52), gi_category ('low' — corrected from 'medium'; GI 52 falls in the low category per University of Sydney GI Database scale of ≤55), glycemic_load_per_serving (34.2), and gi_source are all present. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled egg noodles as the closest analogous approved source. Estimation basis is fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI mapped to 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03315595) with foodon_match_type set to 'closest_parent' (valid permitted value). FoodEx2 code A0CKP used with foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' (valid permitted value). SNOMED CT is null with snomed_ct_match_type 'none' and gap note. No codes are invented; all are defensible. All match_type values are from the permitted set: closest_parent, exact, none.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above the 200-character minimum. It covers what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms are all populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions are present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes', machine key is type2_diabetes), and Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid arthritis is included as an additional condition.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 17 entries covering English variants, Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi forms. aliases_by_language is fully populated for all mandatory Singaporean languages. Self-check performed: every string in aliases_by_language appears verbatim in the flat aliases array. The romanized_local entry 'Mì Vằn Thắn Xá Xíu Mật Ong' from the previous draft was removed as it is a Vietnamese romanization not applicable to this Singaporean Chinese dish; the romanized aliases_by_language array is now empty and consistent with the flat aliases array.
